label: fiction
title: A Face You Don't Forget
charge: Burglary of a home and theft of jewellery
elements: Someone entered the home as a trespasser at night.
The person who did it was the accused.

beats.text: The homeowner is certain: 'I will never forget that face.' She picks the accused from the lineup without a moment's hesitation.
beats.reveal_note: Confidence reads as accuracy, but the two barely correlate. A frightened glimpse in poor light, identified days later, is the classic wrongful-conviction beat.

beats.text: A motorway tolling camera photographed the accused's car 200 km away, timestamped twenty minutes before the break-in.
beats.reveal_note: He cannot be in two places at once. A timestamped photograph outranks a certain memory every time.

beats.text: The accused has a conviction for theft from eight years ago.
beats.reveal_note: It makes him feel like 'the type.' But a past offence is not presence at this one; propensity is not proof.

beats.text: The stolen ring resurfaced in a pawnshop two towns over, logged to a different named seller the following week.
beats.reveal_note: The property trail leads to someone else entirely — quiet, documentary, and hard to argue with.

beats.text: The judge instructs that the confidence of an eyewitness is not a reliable measure of how accurate the identification is.
beats.reveal_note: A caution the whole case turns on: weight the toll photo and the pawn record over the certainty of the pointing finger.

twist: The homeowner isn't lying — she's certain. But certainty isn't accuracy. The accused's car was photographed 200 km away minutes before the break-in, and the ring was pawned by someone else. A confident finger can point at the wrong man.
